paragraphs.body: In the essay, “Blood and Plasma,” Peter Jaworski argues that the collection of blood plasma by voluntary donors should be commercially compensated. He defends his stance by addressing apparent concerns while noting several benefits. First, Jaworski introduces the reader to the issue, speaking about how blood-plasma donations save many lives from multiple illnesses ranging from immune disorders to neurological diseases. He pin-points blood plasma’s critical use in plasma-therapies which treat various illnesses. Jaworski introduces the reader to the issue of the lack of supply of blood-plasma and shortly thereafter he explains that the shortage is only present in countries that prohibit the commercialization of blood plasma donations. He then provides the benefits which include a surplus of blood plasma, which directly leads to better patient outcomes, and a decrease in overall mortality rates, as well as the financial benefit of the donors. Jaworski notes several key arguments against blood plasma donation such as eroding community solidarity, exploitation, and safety.

annotations.text: Jaworski notes several key arguments against blood plasma donation such as eroding community solidarity, exploitation, and safety.

paragraphs.body: Ignorance is associated with a lack of knowledge of some concept or idea. When people don’t know something, they can make uninformed decisions based on false information or maybe just pure feeling or intuition. This can lead to a lot of unnecessary pain and suffering. Making uninformed decisions can be very impactful in a negative way such as with medical, financial, or career choices. In a historical context, being ignorant can lead to an early death. If our ancestors didn’t know how to make a fire or construct a bow, then they would have not survived. Today it is a little less life-threatening for the most part, but it is still relevant that an uninformed decision can have dire consequences especially in the context of something like commercializing blood plasma donation. As a child I was often told or overheard my siblings being told by my mother to, “check yourself before you wreck yourself.” This phrase is dripping with consequentialist ideals. The idea that one must think about something thoroughly before acting is something I have been taught from a very young age. In my own experience as an Emergency Medical Technician, I have seen first-hand how choices lead to consequences. I remember a call I had gotten as a student where the patient was suffering from severe hypovolemic shock. I responded with the ALS unit which stands for advanced life support signifying the presence of an experienced and knowledgeable paramedic, someone with far more training than an EMT, named Scott. I arrived on scene and approached the patient who was already in another ambulance. As the paramedic and I sat down another EMT hastily began to get the cardiac monitor placed on him. Scott stopped the EMT and said, “Give me a few seconds to think.” I looked back at the patient and tried to place myself in Scott’s shoes and think about the patient the way he was. The patient’s physical appearance was sweaty and pale, but he was alert and conscious and only complained of the worst heartburn in his life. We didn’t even have his vital signs yet and Scott seemed to be formulating some elaborate decision. My mind was racing but I really had no idea, could it have been a diabetic emergency? A heart attack maybe? Either way our primary goal as an emergency responder is to bring the patient to a hospital ASAP. If it were up to me, I would have transported him to our local hospital, but Scott turned over to me and said we are going to Williamsport right now. How did he know that this case was severe enough that we need to transport the patient to a level one trauma center? Enroute to the hospital the patient’s blood pressure was dangerously low and we administered one and half liters of fluid which is a lot. We arrived at the hospital and the patient was rushed into life-saving surgery and was diagnosed with an AAD or Ascending Aortic Dissection. This was a very serious situation that could have resulted in a patient dying if Scott made an uninformed decision.

annotations.text: As a child I was often told or overheard my siblings being told by my mother to, “check yourself before you wreck yourself.” This phrase is dripping with consequentialist ideals.

paragraphs.body: Although now that I am thinking about it, Scott seems to be making an uninformed decision in this case and because of his prior knowledge and intuition it just so happened that he made the right decision. In Kant’s view, “Rational capacity is key to decision making.” Kant was a big believer in logic and rationality for sure. Scott is probably a fan of the 18th century philosopher of course due to his use of logic and reasoning in the situation described to make an uninformed decision that led to a positive outcome.

I started playing an old upright piano in my house about 3 or 4 years ago. I had never gone to a music lesson in my life and when I took the required course in 6th grade, I was largely uninterested. In fact, I really didn’t care about listening to music or playing it until I reached high school. It simply wasn’t something I thought about, I was more concerned with running and playing and being outside. I grew up in a ‘music-less world’ so to speak. But you see there is more to the story. My parents are divorced, and my father emigrated to the U.S. from Iran around 40 years ago. My mother was born here in Pennsylvania and grew up in a very different culture compared to my father. In my father’s culture pursuing anything in music would be trivial and a waste of time and he didn’t listen to music very often in the first place, if he did it would oftentimes be Persian music. My mother, on the other hand, was my biggest musical influence growing. I love Dave Matthews and Bob Dylan to this day. I have such vivid memories of Ants Marching and Two-step playing in the background on the long car-rides to school from my mother’s new home next to the river. I digress, I picked up the mandolin last winter and have learned very quickly that I am a quick learner when it comes to picking up instruments. People are amazed I learned so quickly, I already took the college course offered by Pitt for the mandolin and it was truly wonderful. In college I picked up the guitar. Now my friends cannot believe that I have only been playing for four or five months. I am very happy that I started these musical pursuits but I am also saddened. Where was this feeling all my life? Why now? Why did I not take the time to check music out? Just some ideas…
